input.background: Acute lower respiratory tract infection is one of the commonest causes of death among infants and under-5s in developing countries. A simple test is needed to identify those infants with acute respiratory infection who have lower respiratory tract infection (LRI) and should receive antibiotics from those with upper respiratory tract infection (URI). The following data come from a study of the usefulness of the respiratory rate for this purpose in infants (Cherian et al., 1988).
input.data:
\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|}
\hline Respiratory rate (breaths/min) & Number of children (\%) \\
\hline & LRI & URI \\
\hline 0-30 & 1 (1\%) & 16 (11\%) \\
\hline 31-40 & 4 (3\%) & 77 (51\%) \\
\hline 41-50 & 10 (7\%) & 46 (30\%) \\
\hline 51-60 & 41 (29\%) & 9 (6\%) \\
\hline 61+ & 86 (61\%) & 3 (2\%) \\
\hline Total & 142 (100\%) & 151 (100\%) \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
input.question: Construct $2 \times 2$ tables for each of the four cut-offs 30, 40, 50 and 60 breaths $/ \mathrm{min}$ relating low and high respiratory rate to the correct classification (LRI or URI). Which cut-off gives the best balance of sensitivity and specificity? (This is where their sum is a maximum.)

output.answer:
Taking the cut-off as respiratory rates less than or equal to 30, 40, 50 or 60 breaths $/ \mathrm{min}$ gives:

\begin{tabular}{lrr}
\hline Cut-off &  Sensitivity  &  Specificity  \\
\hline 30 & $141 / 142=99 \%$ & $16 / 151=11 \%$ \\
40 & $137 / 142=96 \%$ & $93 / 151=62 \%$ \\
50 & $127 / 142=89 \%$ & $139 / 151=92 \%$ \\
60 & $86 / 142=61 \%$ & $148 / 151=98 \%$ \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

The best cut-off is 50 breaths $/ \mathrm{min}$, with an overall correct assessment for $266 / 293=91 \%$ of infants.

meta_info.chapter: Some common problems in medical research
